wrapUp: The second weekend of the 2026 World Cup turns on depleted squads and high-stakes openers. Brazil began Group C without Neymar, while Japan travel to Dallas shorn of captain Wataru Endo and Kaoru Mitoma for their Netherlands fixture on 14 June. The Dutch themselves are missing Jurrien Timber and Xavi Simons, making Group F one of the tournament's most compelling early tests. Co-hosts the United States have eased Pulisic fitness fears after a precautionary half-time withdrawal, but England's Kansas City arrival was complicated by a training-equipment theft on 13 June. Each story below links to the specific report it is drawn from; squad and fitness details reflect the latest available reporting as of 14 June 2026.

sections.heading: Injuries and fitness doubts
sections.description: Players ruled out, late replacements and day-to-day concerns that could change starting line-ups and squad depth.

stories.headline: Ancelotti confirms Neymar will miss Morocco opener
stories.summary: Carlo Ancelotti said on 13 June that Neymar will not play Brazil's Group C curtain-raiser against Morocco, with the 34-year-old yet to return to full training after a grade-two calf injury. Ancelotti added that Neymar may only rejoin squad training next week, casting doubt on his availability for the Haiti fixture on 20 June. Brazil have already lost Rodrygo (ACL), Estêvão (hamstring) and Éder Militão before the tournament, while right-back Wesley was ruled out with a thigh injury and replaced by Éderson. Vinícius Júnior and Raphinha remain, but the Seleção look thinner in attack than their pedigree suggests.

stories.headline: De la Fuente confirms Yamal is available but unlikely to play 90 minutes
stories.summary: Luis de la Fuente said on 13 June that Lamine Yamal is fit enough to feature against Cape Verde on 15 June after returning from a hamstring injury, though he is not ready for a full match and may begin on the bench. Nico Williams and Víctor Muñoz have also resumed training after missing Spain's Peru friendly. Euro 2024 winners remain without Fermín López, who fractured a metatarsal in May, while Rodri carries a recent ACL history that de la Fuente must manage across a demanding 48-team schedule.

stories.headline: Pulisic plays down calf knock after precautionary half-time withdrawal
stories.summary: Christian Pulisic was substituted at half-time during the United States' 4-1 win over Paraguay on 12 June after taking a kick to his left calf on top of a pre-existing hamstring strain. The AC Milan forward and Mauricio Pochettino both described the change as precautionary, with Pulisic telling reporters it was "nothing" serious. He had set up Folarin Balogun's first goal and was the team's main creative outlet in a dominant opening 45 minutes, so any setback would be felt before the Australia fixture on 19 June.

stories.headline: Canada earn first World Cup point without Davies
stories.summary: Co-hosts Canada drew 1-1 with Bosnia and Herzegovina at BMO Field on 12 June, Cyle Larin equalising in the 78th minute after Jovo Lukic had headed Bosnia ahead. Alphonso Davies did not feature as he continues to recover from a hamstring injury sustained in May, though Jesse Marsch said an MRI showed encouraging progress and Davies may return later in Group B. Centre-back Moïse Bombito was available after winning his fitness race. The point was Canada's first in World Cup history, ending a run of six consecutive group-stage defeats.

stories.headline: Richards starts as United States thump Paraguay 4-1
stories.summary: Johnny Cardoso was ruled out after ankle surgery, but Mauricio Pochettino named Chris Richards at centre-back for the 4-1 Group D opener on 12 June, the Crystal Palace defender having recovered from torn ankle ligaments in May. Folarin Balogun scored twice in a joint-record World Cup win for the hosts. Pochettino had confirmed all 26 remaining players were available beforehand, with Group D also featuring Australia and Türkiye.

stories.headline: Mexico win opener but lose Montes to red card after fitness gamble
stories.summary: Javier Aguirre's side beat South Africa 2-0 in the tournament's opening match at the Azteca, yet the night was marred by three red cards, the most in a World Cup opener. César Montes, one of four players Aguirre had been managing through muscle complaints, was sent off in stoppage time and will miss the South Korea fixture on 18 June. Edson Álvarez, Luis Chávez and Alexis Vega all featured, suggesting Aguirre's pre-tournament fitness gambles largely paid off for the first game.

stories.headline: Morocco lose Ezzalzouli and Aguerd on eve of Brazil opener
stories.summary: FIFA confirmed late squad changes after forward Abde Ezzalzouli suffered a knee ligament injury colliding with team-mate Chadi Riad in the Norway friendly, and centre-back Nayef Aguerd failed to recover from a long-standing groin problem. Amine Sbai and Marwane Saadane were called up as replacements. Morocco had hoped both could be nursed through the group stage but replaced them before the 24-hour registration deadline ahead of their Group C opener against Brazil on 13 June.

stories.headline: Netherlands lose Timber and Simons as Verbruggen faces race for Japan opener
stories.summary: Jurrien Timber has been ruled out with a groin strain despite playing in Arsenal's Champions League final, joining ACL casualties Xavi Simons and Jerdy Schouten plus Matthijs de Ligt, who failed to recover from a back problem. Ronald Koeman must also monitor goalkeeper Bart Verbruggen, who missed training with a hip issue after a collision against Uzbekistan. The Dutch open Group F against Japan in Dallas on 14 June without several planned starters.

stories.headline: South Korea fear Kim Tae-hyun could miss entire group stage
stories.summary: Starting centre-back Kim Tae-hyun injured his ankle in training and may sit out all three Group A fixtures against Czechia, Mexico and South Africa. Hong Myung-bo's side had already lost Jo Yu-min during the warm-up window. With Kim Min-jae the only established aerial presence left, South Korea's defensive plan against Mexico's direct play is under strain before a ball is kicked.

sections.heading: Squad selection and tactical reshuffles
sections.description: Omissions, late call-ups and manager decisions that reveal how teams plan to navigate the expanded format.

stories.headline: Argentina lose Balerdi as Senesi confirmed as replacement
stories.summary: Leonardo Balerdi suffered a soleus muscle tear in training and the AFA confirmed he would miss the World Cup. On 12 June, the federation announced that Marcos Senesi would take his place, ending speculation that Lionel Scaloni might recall a midfielder instead. Senesi, who has joined Tottenham on a free transfer, adds cover alongside Cristian Romero, Lisandro Martínez and Nicolás Otamendi. Argentina open Group J against Algeria on 16 June.

stories.headline: Endo withdraws and retires as Machino called up before Netherlands opener
stories.summary: Japan captain Wataru Endo pulled out of the World Cup on 11 June after failing to recover from a foot injury sustained at Liverpool in February, and announced his immediate retirement from international football with 73 caps. Borussia Mönchengladbach forward Shūto Machino was named as his replacement, while Ajax defender Kō Itakura takes over as captain. Japan had already lost Kaoru Mitoma and Takumi Minamino to injury, thinning a midfield that must face the Netherlands, Sweden and Tunisia in Group F.

sections.heading: Disruption and preparation issues
sections.description: Travel, visa and logistical problems that could affect match readiness rather than just headlines.

stories.headline: England training gear stolen en route to Kansas City base
stories.summary: Balls, boots and other equipment were stolen from a vehicle transferring England's kit from their Florida pre-tournament camp to Swope Soccer Village in Kansas City on 13 June, just before the squad were due to arrive. Kansas City police arrested two suspects and most items were subsequently recovered, but the incident disrupted setup ahead of Thomas Tuchel's first full training day on 14 June. England open Group L against Croatia in Dallas on 17 June.

stories.headline: Iraq squad members face lengthy US border checks
stories.summary: Striker Aymen Hussein was detained for nearly seven hours at Chicago O'Hare before being admitted, while team photographer Talal Salah was held for more than ten hours and ultimately denied entry. Iraq open Group I against France on 16 June. Extended border processing for staff and players adds uncertainty to a squad already tasked with navigating a group containing France, Senegal and Norway.

sections.heading: Analysis and tournament outlook
sections.description: Deeper previews on favourites, group dynamics and how the 48-team format changes the path to the final.

stories.headline: Group F opener pits depleted Netherlands against reshaped Japan
stories.summary: Ronald Koeman's side face Hajime Moriyasu's Japan in Dallas on 14 June in a fixture that could shape the race for top spot in one of the tournament's most competitive groups. The Dutch are without Timber, Simons and de Ligt but retain a strong spine of Virgil van Dijk, Frenkie de Jong and Cody Gakpo, while Japan arrive on a six-match winning run despite losing Endo, Mitoma and Minamino. Sweden and Tunisia complete a pool where squad depth and rotation capacity may matter more than in previous editions.

stories.headline: Group C opener pits depleted Brazil against reshaped Morocco
stories.summary: Pre-tournament analysis had flagged Group C as one of the toughest pools, and the opening fixture was further reshaped by injuries on both sides. Ancelotti confirmed on 13 June that Neymar would miss the curtain-raiser and may not return until the Haiti match, while Brazil are also without Rodrygo, Estêvão and Wesley. Morocco have replaced Ezzalzouli and Aguerd with less familiar names, though Noussair Mazraoui returned to full training after a shoulder scare. Morocco's semi-final pedigree from Qatar 2022 still makes them a credible test for Ancelotti's rebuilt attack.

stories.headline: Opta flags record red-card tally and format mismatches after Mexico opener
stories.summary: Opta Analyst's review of Mexico's 2-0 win over South Africa notes three red cards in a single match, a World Cup record for an opener and already one fewer than the entire 2022 tournament produced. South Africa managed just 0.07 xG from three shots despite Mexico ranking only 21st against their 60th in the Power Rankings, suggesting the 48-team format could yield more one-sided scorelines. César Montes' late dismissal also rules the centre-back out of Mexico's South Korea tie on 18 June, adding a squad consequence to the disciplinary chaos.
